structured_choices.data_source: Resting-state fMRI scans from a longitudinal Alzheimer's disease cohort study (5,000 scans, 5-year collection period). We assume access to: preprocessed BOLD timeseries (motion-corrected, registered to standard space), cognitive test scores (MMSE, MoCA, or equivalent), clinical diagnosis labels (from neurologist consensus or biomarker confirmation), and demographic metadata (age, sex, education, APOE status, comorbidities). Data collection site, scanner model, and acquisition parameters are documented.
structured_choices.label_definition: Multi-class outcome: clinical Alzheimer's disease stage (cognitively normal [CN], mild cognitive impairment [MCI], Alzheimer's disease dementia [ADD]). Labels are derived from clinician consensus diagnosis at baseline or longitudinal follow-up, validated against neuropsychological test batteries and, where available, amyloid/tau biomarkers. Only scans with unambiguous labels ≥6 months post-clinical assessment are included to minimize label noise.
structured_choices.split_method: Stratified group-based split with temporal separation within each group. First, stratify by clinical stage (CN/MCI/ADD) to preserve class distribution. Then, within each stratum, randomly assign participants (not scans) to train (70%), validation (15%), and test (15%) cohorts. All scans from a single participant remain in one set to prevent information leakage from longitudinal follow-ups. This avoids data leakage from the same individual appearing across sets and preserves clinically meaningful heterogeneity. Secondary site-stratified splits are performed post-hoc to verify generalization across scanner hardware and acquisition protocols.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: Image preprocessing (skull stripping, motion correction, registration to MNI space) is applied uniformly to all scans before any analysis. Functional connectivity matrix computation and dimensionality reduction (PCA or ICA) are fit exclusively on the training set. Standardization (z-score normalization) of extracted features is fit on training data and applied identically to validation and test sets. Clustering hyperparameters (e.g., number of clusters, distance metric) are selected using the validation set only, with final evaluation on the held-out test set.
structured_choices.features: Two feature pipelines are constructed in parallel: (1) Unsupervised: Resting-state functional connectivity matrices computed from preprocessed BOLD timeseries, reduced via PCA/ICA to 50–100 components, then clustered using k-means or hierarchical clustering (k selected via silhouette score on validation data). Cluster assignments serve as categorical features for downstream classification. (2) Baseline (cognitive tests): Age-adjusted MMSE, MoCA, or composite z-scored cognitive domain scores (memory, executive function, visuospatial). Both pipelines include demographic covariates (age, sex, education) to control for confounds.
structured_choices.model: Primary model: Logistic regression or gradient boosted classifier (XGBoost) trained on unsupervised cluster features (+ demographics) to predict clinical stage. This choice decouples the unsupervised clustering quality from the downstream classification task and allows fair comparison to cognitive test baseline. Model hyperparameters are tuned on the validation set (learning rate, regularization strength, number of trees) using grid search with 5-fold cross-validation within the training set.
structured_choices.baseline: Logistic regression trained on cognitive test scores (MMSE/MoCA + demographic covariates) to predict clinical stage. This baseline represents current clinical practice. Hyperparameter tuning effort is matched to the primary model: regularization strength (L2) is tuned on the validation set using the same grid search procedure. The baseline receives identical preprocessing and evaluation conditions.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Weighted F1 score (macro-averaged across the three classes: CN, MCI, ADD). Justification: Weighted F1 balances precision and recall and accounts for class imbalance typical in Alzheimer's cohorts. Macro-averaging ensures equal emphasis on rare classes (e.g., AD dementia), which is clinically important—missing AD cases is more costly than false positives. A clinically relevant threshold is ≥0.75 F1 to establish practical superiority over cognitive tests.
secondary_metrics: Weighted precision and recall (per-class to identify any stage-specific performance gaps)
ROC-AUC for CN vs. non-CN and MCI vs. ADD (to assess discriminative ability at key clinical boundaries)
Confusion matrix to identify misclassification patterns (e.g., MCI vs. CN confusion suggests subthreshold cognitive decline detection)
Balanced accuracy (to verify performance is not driven by the largest class)
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Validation set (15% of data, stratified by stage and site). Hyperparameter grids are searched using 5-fold stratified cross-validation within the training set (to avoid double-dipping on validation data). The validation set is used once per candidate model configuration to select the final hyperparameters. The test set is held out completely until final reporting.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set is untouched during model development and hyperparameter selection. After final models are trained (primary and baseline), both are evaluated once on the test set under identical conditions. Test set performance is reported with 95% confidence intervals (via bootstrap or cross-validation on the test set itself is not permitted; instead, intervals are computed post-hoc from test predictions).
structured_choices.confound_controls: Demographic covariates (age, sex, education, APOE ε4 status) are included as features in both primary and baseline models to control for confounding. Stratified splits ensure balanced age and sex distributions across train/val/test. Post-hoc subgroup analyses stratify test set performance by age quartile, sex, and site to check for differential accuracy. Any significant interactions (e.g., higher accuracy in younger patients) are documented and interpreted as either genuine population effects or residual confounding. Analysis of covariance (ANCOVA) adjusting for age/sex is used to confirm primary findings are robust to confounding.

design_narrative:
# Experiment Design: Unsupervised fMRI Clustering vs. Cognitive Tests for Alzheimer's Classification

## 1. Data and Label Definition

We will use resting-state fMRI scans from a 5-year longitudinal Alzheimer's cohort (n ≈ 5,000 scans, representing ≈ 1,000–1,500 unique participants). Preprocessing will be standardized: motion correction, skull stripping, and registration to MNI152 template. Clinical labels (cognitively normal, mild cognitive impairment, Alzheimer's dementia) are assigned by consensus of two independent neurologists, supplemented by neuropsychological batteries and, where available, CSF or PET biomarkers for validation. Labels are stable only if assigned ≥6 months prior to scan acquisition, ensuring minimal label noise.

## 2. Data Splitting Strategy

We employ a **stratified, group-based split with temporal validation checks**. Participants (not individual scans) are randomly allocated: 70% train, 15% validation, 15% test. Stratification is performed by clinical stage to preserve class distribution across all three sets. All scans from a single participant remain in one set, preventing information leakage from longitudinal follow-ups. After random assignment, we verify that train/val/test are balanced by age, sex, scanner site, and acquisition protocol. This approach preserves clinical realism—the model must generalize to unseen individuals, not simply memorize patterns from repeated scans of known patients.

## 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

**Two parallel pipelines:**

**Unsupervised (Primary):** From preprocessed BOLD timeseries, we compute whole-brain functional connectivity matrices (Pearson correlation between 400 ROIs in the Schaefer atlas). To reduce dimensionality while preserving meaningful structure, we apply PCA or ICA on the *training set only* to extract 50–100 independent or principal components. We then apply k-means clustering (k = 3, 4, or 5; k selected via silhouette score on the validation set) to assign each scan to a cluster. Cluster assignments become categorical features for downstream classification.

**Cognitive Test (Baseline):** Age-adjusted MMSE or MoCA total scores, supplemented by domain-specific z-scored composites (memory, executive function, visuospatial reasoning) derived from comprehensive neuropsych batteries.

**Confound Handling:** Age, sex, education, and APOE ε4 status are included as numerical/categorical covariates in both pipelines. Feature standardization (z-score) is fit on the training set and applied identically to validation and test.

## 4. Model and Baseline

**Primary Model:** Logistic regression or XGBoost classifier trained on (unsupervised cluster features + demographics) to predict clinical stage. Hyperparameters (L2 regularization, learning rate, tree depth) are tuned on the validation set via grid search with stratified 5-fold CV within the training set.

**Baseline Model:** Logistic regression trained on (cognitive test scores + demographics). Hyperparameter tuning is matched in effort to the primary model. This baseline represents clinical standard-of-care and provides fair comparison—if unsupervised neuroimaging cannot outperform well-tuned cognitive tests, the hypothesis is not supported.

## 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary:** Weighted macro F1 score across three classes. This metric balances precision and recall, handles class imbalance, and ensures rare classes (e.g., AD dementia) are weighted equally—clinically appropriate because missing an AD case is costly. Threshold for superiority: ≥0.75 F1 for primary model, with primary model F1 ≥ 0.05 points above baseline.

**Secondary:**
- Per-class precision, recall, and F1
- Weighted ROC-AUC (one-vs-rest for each class)
- Confusion matrices to identify misclassification patterns
- Balanced accuracy to confirm performance is not driven by largest class

## 6. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameter tuning occurs entirely within training + validation data. The validation set is used once to select the best configuration. The test set is held out completely and evaluated only after final model selection. No test set peeking occurs.

## 7. Confound and Bias Controls

- Demographic covariates are included as features in both models.
- Stratified splits ensure balanced demographics across train/val/test.
- Post-hoc subgroup analyses stratify test performance by age quartile, sex, and scanner site to detect differential accuracy.
- ANCOVA is used to confirm primary findings remain robust after adjusting for age and sex.
- Site-based secondary splits test generalization across scanner hardware.

## 8. Scope of Claims

This design establishes whether unsupervised fMRI clustering features improve multi-class clinical classification over cognitive tests *in this cohort*. It does not establish causality, optimal clustering parameters (those are tuned on validation data), or generalization beyond the 5-year enrollment period or acquisition protocols represented. If the primary model outperforms the baseline with statistical significance (p < 0.05 on the test set via paired-sample t-tests or McNemar test), the hypothesis is supported conditionally on the cohort and design.
